text: Alright, let's go. Number 10. Kepler-20e, the first sub-Earth. When astronomers first began hunting for planets beyond our solar system, they were finding monsters. Massive gas giants, many times the size of Jupiter, whipping around their host stars in a matter of days. These hot Jupiters were the easiest to spot because their immense gravity tugged fiercely on their stars, and their massive silhouettes blocked out substantial starlight. For years, the scientific community wondered if our technology would ever be sensitive enough to detect something as small as Earth, let alone something smaller. The psychological barrier was immense. We were essentially trying to spot a grain of sand passing in front of a stadium spotlight from hundreds of miles away. Then, in December 2011, the narrative of exoplanetary science completely shattered. Astronomers analyzing data from the Kepler Space Telescope announced the discovery of Kepler-20e, a planet orbiting a sun-like star roughly 1,000 light-years away in the constellation Lyra. What made this announcement monumental was the planet's radius. Measuring in at roughly 0.87 times the radius of Earth, Kepler-20e was officially the very first exoplanet discovered that was smaller than our own homeworld. It is slightly smaller than Venus, making it a true sub-Earth, but if you are picturing a temperate Venusian twin or a secondary Earth, you need to abandon that image immediately. Kepler-20e is trapped in a remarkably tight orbit, circling its host star every 6.1 days. At this proximity, the planet is completely tidally locked, presenting the same scorched face to its star for eternity. The surface temperature on that day side reaches a staggering 1,400 degrees Fahrenheit, or over 760 degrees Celsius. That is hot enough to melt glass and completely liquefy metals like zinc and lead. What makes Kepler-20e so unsettling is its sheer hostility combined with its groundbreaking status. It proved that the universe does indeed manufacture small, rocky worlds around sun-like stars, and more importantly, it proved that humanity had finally developed the technological prowess to find them. Yet this historic first glimpse at a sub-Earth revealed a completely sterilized, radiation-blasted rock. It served as a stark, humbling reminder that just because a planet is terrestrial and rocky, it does not mean the cosmos has designed it for life. It is a dead cinder, serving as our first true stepping stone into the realm of tiny worlds. In early 2017, the astronomical community was rocked by the discovery of the Trappist-1 system, located just 40 light-years away from Earth. Instead of a sun-like star, the center of this system is an ultra-cool red dwarf, a star so small and dim that it emits barely a fraction of our sun's energy. What makes this system terrifyingly unique is its architecture. Astronomers found not one, not two, but seven roughly Earth-sized planets crammed into an orbital space smaller than the distance between Mercury and our sun. The planets are so close together that they constantly gravitationally interact, their orbits perfectly synchronized in a complex cosmic dance. Among this crowded family is Trappist-1D, one of the smallest planets in the entire system, with a radius just under 0.78 times that of Earth and a mass only about 30% of our homeworld, it is a true cosmic featherweight. It completes an entire orbit around its dim red star in just 4.05 days. But what makes Trappist-1D profoundly fascinating, and perhaps a bit eerie, is its location. It sits precisely at the inner edge of the star's habitable zone, that theoretical region where temperatures might just be right for liquid water to pool on the surface. Imagine standing on the surface of Trappist-1D. The sky above you would be an alien nightmare of massive looming spheres. Because the planets are packed so tightly, its neighboring worlds would frequently pass overhead, appearing much larger and far more detailed than our moon does from Earth. You would be able to see weather patterns and geological features on neighboring planets with the naked eye. The sun in the sky would be a swollen, dull, crimson orb, casting long, bloody shadows across a tidally locked landscape where one side is trapped in perpetual twilight and the other in eternal night. The existence of Trappist-1D forces us to confront a bizarre reality about planetary formation. We are used to vast, empty spaces between planets, separated by millions of miles of dark void. But Trappist-1D exists in a claustrophobic neighborhood. If life somehow managed to take hold on this diminutive world, it would evolve under a sky that feels unnervingly close, constantly watched by the giant, silent faces of its planetary siblings. It challenges our understanding of what a solar system should look like, proving that the universe is perfectly capable of packing an entire family of tiny, rocky worlds into a space we previously thought was too dangerously close to a star to survive. 8. Kepler-62C, The Scorched Inner Seed When NASA announced the discovery of the Kepler-62 system, located about 1,200 light-years away in the constellation Lyra, the media focused heavily on the two outermost planets, Kepler-62E and Kepler-62F. Both were super-Earths, located squarely within the habitable zone, sparking intense speculation about global oceans and the potential for extraterrestrial biology. But while everyone was looking at the habitable zone, they ignored the system's inner architect, a diminutive, roasted world known as Kepler-62C. Kepler-62C is tiny, measuring in at just 0.54 times the radius of Earth. This puts it squarely in the size category of Mars. However, unlike our cold, rusted neighbor, Kepler-62C is subjected to an absolute inferno. It orbits its host star in roughly 12 days, meaning it sits incredibly close to the stellar furnace. The amount of stellar radiation it receives guarantees that any atmosphere it might have once possessed was violently stripped away billions of years ago by relentless stellar winds and extreme heat. It is a naked planetary core, baked by the intense, unyielding glare of its parent star. What makes Kepler-62C essential to understand, and deeply unsettling, is what it represents about the violent mechanics of planetary formation. Current models suggest that when planetary systems form from a swirling disc of gas and dust, the planets closest to the star take an absolute beating. They are bombarded by radiation, stripped of their volatile elements like water and atmospheric gases, and left as dense, dry husks. Kepler-62C is the charred intercede of a system that managed to preserve potentially life-bearing worlds further out. It is the sacrificial lamb of the Kepler-62 system. Looking at this tiny world forces astronomers to realize that the majestic, potentially habitable planets we so desperately search for are often accompanied by these scorched, diminutive siblings. They are the silent, baked remnants of a solar system's chaotic youth. If you were to stand on the barren, likely magma-cracked surface of Kepler-62C, you would look out into the dark and know that the biological potential of your solar system was entirely reserved for the distant, cooler worlds, while your planet was destined to burn for billions of years in solitary confinement. 7. Kepler-138b, the Mars-sized featherweight Finding a planet smaller than Earth is an incredible technological achievement on its own, usually requiring astronomers to measure the infinitesimal dip in a star's light as the planet passes in front of it. But determining the mass of something that small is a completely different and infinitely more difficult challenge. For years, tiny exoplanets were just shadows. We knew their physical size based on the light they blocked, but we had absolutely no idea how heavy they were. That changed with a fascinating diminutive world known as Kepler-138b, located 218 light years away in the constellation Lyra. Kepler-138b has a radius of roughly 0.64 times that of Earth, making it incredibly close in size to the planet Mars. But what makes this world genuinely mind-bending is how scientists manage to weigh it. Kepler-138b is part of a multi-planet system orbiting a red dwarf star. Because these planets orbit relatively close to one another, their gravities gently tug on each other as they pass by. This cosmic dance causes slight variations in the exact timing of their transits across the star. By measuring these minute transit timing variations over several years, astronomers could calculate the gravitational pull and therefore the mass of Kepler-138b. The results were astonishing. Kepler-138b is an absolute featherweight. Its mass is only about 6.7% that of Earth. When you combine its Mars-like size with its incredibly low mass, you get a density that strongly suggests it is a purely rocky world, entirely devoid of any thick, massive atmosphere or deep global oceans. It is a dead, silent rock, tumbling through the void around a dim red star, completing an orbit every 10 days. The discovery and measurement of Kepler-138b is a monumental milestone, but it also paints a bleak picture of the sheer abundance of dead worlds in our galaxy. It proves that the universe is highly efficient at manufacturing terrestrial, rocky bodies that are simply too small to hold onto an atmosphere or support the complex chemistry needed for life. It is a world where the gravity is so weak and the environment so stripped that nothing could ever take hold. Kepler-138b is a testament to the fact that for every Earth-like oasis we might one day find, there are countless thousands of these tiny barren stones endlessly circling their stars in the absolute silence of the cosmic dark. Number 6. GJ-367b, the heavyweight iron cannonball. If Kepler-138b is a cosmic featherweight, our next entry is the exact opposite. Located roughly 31 light-years away from Earth, orbiting an unassuming red dwarf star is a planet that shouldn't mathematically exist in its current form. Designated GJ-367b, this world has a radius of about 0.72 times that of Earth. It is a sub-Earth, firmly smaller than our planet. But when astronomers used the radial velocity method to measure the gravitational wobble of its host star, they discovered something absolutely terrifying about this tiny world. Despite being only about 72% of Earth's size, GJ-367b contains 55% of Earth's total mass. When you calculate the density required to pack that much mass into such a small volume, the result is staggering. This planet is almost as dense as pure iron. In fact, scientists estimate that 86% of the entire planet is composed of a solid iron core. To put that in perspective, Earth's iron core makes up only about a third of its total mass. GJ-367b isn't just a rocky planet, it is essentially a naked, giant ball of metal flying through space. Adding to the horror of this world is its orbit. It zips around its host star in a mind-bending 7.7 hours. An entire year on this planet lasts less than a standard human workday. Because it is so unimaginably close to the star, it is tidally locked, and the dayside is subjected to relentless searing radiation. The surface temperatures exceed 2700 degrees Fahrenheit, or 1500 degrees Celsius. At these temperatures, the surface of this metallic world isn't solid rock. It is a global, churning ocean of molten iron and vaporized rock. But the most disturbing aspect of GJ-367b is how it came to be. Planets do not naturally form as pure iron cores. They form with thick, rocky mantles and outer crusts. For GJ-367b to exist in its current state, it must have been subjected to an apocalyptic event. Astronomers believe it likely suffered a catastrophic, unimaginable collision with another massive planetary body early in its history. An impact so violent that it literally ripped the entire outer, rocky mantle off the planet, leaving only the dense, metallic heart behind. It is a scarred, molten survivor of a cosmic hit and run, a heavy iron cannonball eternally roasting in the fires of its parent star. 5. Kepler-444b, the ancient relic When we look up at the stars, we are looking back in time, but certain discoveries force us to confront the sheer, crushing weight of deep cosmic history. Located 117 light years away in the constellation Lyra is the Kepler-444 system. The host star is relatively small, but its most defining characteristic is its age. Kepler-444 is estimated to be an astonishing 11.2 billion years old. Our entire solar system, our sun, the earth, and everything we have ever known is only about 4.6 billion years old. This star was already ancient when our planet was nothing more than a swirling cloud of scattered dust. Orbiting this ancient stellar patriarch is a tightly packed system of five planets, and the innermost of these is a diminutive world named Kepler-444b. This planet is incredibly small, measuring just 0.4 times the radius of earth. It is essentially the size of Mercury. But what makes Kepler-444b genuinely unsettling is its status as a cosmic fossil. Because the star is 11.2 billion years old, the planets that orbit it must be nearly the same age. This means Kepler-444b formed when the universe itself was only about 2.5 billion years old. Before this discovery, many astrophysicists believed that the early universe simply didn't possess enough heavy elements, like iron, silicon, and carbon, to form rocky terrestrial planets. These elements had to be forged in the cores of the first generations of stars, and then scattered across the cosmos via supernovae. Kepler-444b proves that the universe was building rocky worlds far earlier than we ever dared to imagine. It is a relic from the dawn of galactic history. But any romantic notions of ancient, billion-year-old alien civilizations must be abandoned. Kepler-444b orbits its star in a mere 3.6 days. It is so close to the stellar surface that it is completely sterilized, an airless, scorched rock incapable of supporting any biology. Yet, its mere existence is haunting. It means that the universe had the ingredients and the mechanics to build rocky worlds over 6 billion years before Earth even existed. It forces us to ask, if rocky planets have been around for over 11 billion years, how many billions of years of biological potential have already risen and fallen in the cold, dark of the galaxy, leaving only these ancient, silent stones behind. Number 4. Kepler-42d, the diminutive world of a miniature star. The universe has a strange sense of scale. Sometimes, it builds massive, sprawling systems with giant stars and Jupiter-sized planets. Other times, it builds systems that feel like miniature, scaled-down models of what a solar system should be. Located 126 light-years away in the constellation Cygnus is the star Kepler-42, originally designated as KOI-961. This host star is a red dwarf, but it is an exceptionally small one. It possesses only about 13% of the mass of our sun, making it barely larger than the planet Jupiter. If it had been just a fraction smaller, it would not have had enough mass to ignite nuclear fusion, and it would have been a failed star, a brown dwarf. Orbiting this miniature stellar engine is a system of three tiny planets, the smallest of which is Kepler-42d. This diminutive world has a radius of just 0.57 times that of Earth, making it roughly the size of Mars. It completes an entire orbit around its tiny host star in an incredibly brief 1.8 days. The entire planetary system of Kepler-42 is so incredibly compact that all three of its planets orbit at a distance far closer to their star than Jupiter's moons orbit around Jupiter. What makes Kepler-42d so fascinating and somewhat eerie is how it blurs the lines of cosmic definition. The system looks less like a traditional solar system and more like a gas giant with a collection of rocky moons. Yet, the central body is undergoing fusion. It is a true star, and therefore, Kepler-42d is a true planet. It shows us that planetary systems are not a one-size-fits-all phenomenon. The universe is capable of taking the exact same physical laws that built our expansive solar system and applying them to a microscopic scale. Standing on Kepler-42d would be a claustrophobic nightmare. The sun in your sky would be a massive, dim, red sphere, taking up a terrifying amount of the horizon due to your extreme proximity to it. The surface is likely a barren, rocky wasteland, constantly bombarded by the flare activity typical of volatile red dwarf stars. It is a miniature world in a miniature system, proving that nature's ability to create terrestrial planets extends to the absolute smallest, most extreme stellar environments imaginable. Number 3. PSRB-1257-12B Draugr, the undead pulsar planet. If you want to find the most terrifying, hostile, and utterly impossible planets in the universe, you have to look in the graveyards of dead stars. In 1992, astronomers Alexander Walshian and Dale Frail made history by discovering the very first confirmed exoplanets. But they didn't find them orbiting a normal, sun-like star. They found them orbiting a pulsar. A pulsar is the rapidly spinning, ultra-dense, highly magnetic corpse of a massive star that ended its life in a catastrophic supernova explosion. When a star goes supernova, the sheer, violent output of energy should completely obliterate any planets in the inner system and fling the outer planets into deep space. Nothing should survive. Yet, orbiting the pulsar, PSRB-1257-12, located 2,300 light-years away in the constellation Virgo, is a system of three planets. The innermost and smallest of these is officially named Draugr, after the undead creatures of Norse mythology. Draugr is the least massive exoplanet ever discovered. Its mass is roughly 0.02 times that of Earth, making it about twice as massive as our moon. Based on mass-radius relationships, scientists estimate its radius is around 0.338 times that of Earth. The existence of Draugr is a cosmic horror story. How does a planet exist around a dead star that exploded? The leading theory is incredibly grim. Astronomers believe that Draugr and its siblings are second-generation planets. They did not survive the supernova. Instead, they were formed from the ashes and shattered debris of the original star system. After the explosion, a disk of heavy elements and pulverized rock settled into orbit around the undead pulsar corpse. And over millions of years, this debris coalesced to form new, Frankenstein-like worlds out of the remains of the old. Draugr exists in an environment that defies comprehension. The pulsar at orbit spins rapidly, sweeping the planet with relentless, high-energy beams of sterilizing radiation and intense magnetic fields. There is no visible light, no warmth, just the cold, dead strobe of a spinning stellar corpse. Draugr is bathed in an endless torrent of x-rays and charged particles. It is a zombie world, born from death, orbiting death, in an environment so profoundly hostile that the very concept of biology is an absurdity. It serves as a dark reminder that planetary formation doesn't require a nurturing environment. It just requires gravity and debris. Number 2 WD1145 plus 017b, the disintegrating ghost world. While discovering ancient planets or zombie worlds is deeply unsettling, nothing quite compares to the horror of watching a planet actively being executed in real time. Located approximately 570 light-years away in the constellation Virgo is a white dwarf star known as WD1145 plus 017. A white dwarf is the end state of a star similar to our sun. After it exhausts its nuclear fuel, it swells into a red giant, swallowing its inner planets before shedding its outer layers and leaving behind a dense, glowing core. In 2015, astronomers observing this white dwarf noticed something completely bizarre. The starlight was dipping periodically, but not in the clean, predictable U-shape typical of a solid planet transiting a star. The light curves were jagged, asymmetrical, and constantly changing. Through further analysis, scientists realized they were not just looking at a planet, they were looking at a massive, trailing cloud of dust and metallic gas. Hidden within that cloud is WD1145 plus 017b, a tiny planetesimal with an estimated radius of just 0.03 times that of Earth, making it roughly the size of a large asteroid or a small moon. But this tiny world is doomed. When the host star died and became a white dwarf, the gravitational dynamics of the entire system were thrown into chaos. This chaos perturbed the orbit of WD1145 plus 017b, sending it spiraling inward on a death trajectory. It now orbits the white dwarf in just 4.5 hours. At this incredibly close proximity, the intense gravity of the ultra-dense white dwarf is exerting immense tidal forces on the tiny world. These forces are literally ripping the planet apart piece by piece. The jagged light curves astronomers observe are the result of massive chunks of rock fracturing off the planetesimal and grinding themselves into dust, creating a comet-like tail of debris that bleeds away into space. The surface is simultaneously being vaporized by the intense heat of the white dwarf. We are witnessing the gruesome, protracted death of a world. Over the next few million years, WD1145 plus 017b will be completely pulverized and consumed by its dead star. It is a sobering, terrifying preview of what might ultimately happen to the remaining asteroids and moons in our own solar system billions of years from now, when our sun finally dies and the system descends into gravitational madness. 1. Kepler-37b, the lunar-sized record holder Throughout this journey, we have explored sub-Earths, dead iron cores, and disintegrating planetesimals. But when it comes to intact, confirmed planets orbiting a stable, main-sequence star, one diminutive world holds the absolute record for the smallest exoplanet ever discovered. Located 209 light-years away in the constellation Lyra, orbiting a star very similar to our own sun is a tiny rock named Kepler-37b. Discovered in 2013, Kepler-37b represents the absolute pinnacle of the Kepler space telescope's observational capabilities. Finding this planet required measuring a decrease in the host star's brightness of just 22 parts per million. It is the astronomical equivalent of detecting a nat flying across the beam of a search light from several miles away. The precision required to confirm its existence was staggering, aided by the relatively quiet nature of the host star. When the calculations were finalized, the astronomical community was astounded. Kepler-37b has a radius of roughly 0.31 times that of Earth. This makes it slightly larger than our own moon, and actually smaller than the planet Mercury. To put that into perspective, if you were to stand on the surface of Kepler-37b, the horizon would curve sharply away from you, and the gravity would be so weak that you could likely leap immense distances with a single step. But you wouldn't want to stand there. Kepler-37b is locked in a punishingly tight orbit, whipping around its sun-like star in just 13.4 days. Because of its tiny mass and extreme proximity to the star, it is completely impossible for it to hold on to any semblance of an atmosphere. It is a naked rock, exposed to the vacuum of space. The surface temperature on the day side is estimated to be over 800 degrees Fahrenheit, or roughly 425 degrees Celsius. That is hot enough to completely melt zinc and turn the surface into a baked, sterile wasteland. Kepler-37b is the ultimate testament to the diversity of the cosmos. It proves that the universe's planetary assembly line doesn't just produce massive gas giants or Earth-sized spheres, it produces billions of tiny, insignificant pebbles. Worlds smaller than our own moon, endlessly circling their stars, silent, barren, and utterly indifferent to our existence. It is the smallest planet we have ever found around a living star, a tiny, glowing ember hidden in the overwhelming glare of the cosmic dark. I'll be sharing more similar videos in the future, so subscribe to stay tuned.
